step.title: Draft Writing Plan
step.goal: For each section in the Structure Outline, decide writing order, subagent dispatch needs, length estimate. Define review checkpoints.

substep.title: Decide Writing Order
substep.action:
Sections-first ordering principle: write the foundational sections first (Problem Statement, Behaviors, Decisions), then the dependent sections (Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, which read against the foundations).
General order heuristic: 1. Sections that LOCK content the others build on: - Problem statement / context - Decisions linked (DR references with brief summaries) 2. Sections that DESCRIBE the behavior: - User stories / behaviors / API contracts - Edge cases 3. Sections that CONSTRAIN: - Acceptance criteria - Out of scope - Risk areas 4. Sections that REFER to other content: - Open questions - Future work - Cross-references
If the Structure Outline uses Parts, the writing order usually follows Part order (Part 1, Part 2, ...) but within each Part the order still follows foundations-first within that Part's section set.
Store as {{writing_order}}: list of section names in writing order.

substep.title: Identify Subagent Dispatch Needs
substep.action:
For each section, decide whether ticket-write will need fresh subagent dispatch to fill it. Common cases:
- **Already covered by research file**: no new dispatch. The ticket-write step pulls from existing findings. - **Needs codebase-pattern-finder**: e.g., "Test Conventions" section needs to find how tests are structured in this repo. - **Needs codebase-locator**: e.g., "Files Touched" or "Existing API Surfaces" — locate the targets. - **Needs thoughts-locator + thoughts-analyzer**: e.g., a section that references prior decisions or research from unrelated tickets. - **Needs web-search-researcher (with user approval)**: rare at write-time; usually web research happens in ticket-grill.
For each section, store as {{dispatch_plan[section_name]}}: {needs_dispatch: bool, subagent: , prompt_hint: }.
Cap: subagents per section should be 0 or 1 typically. Multi-subagent dispatch per section is a sign the section scope is too broad — surface as a structure concern (could require loop-back to ticket-structure).

substep.title: Estimate Section Lengths
substep.action:
Rough length estimates per section (in lines, not characters):
- Short / declarative: 10-30 lines (one-liner sections like "Out of Scope" with a bullet list) - Medium / explanatory: 30-100 lines (typical narrative sections) - Long / detailed: 100-300 lines (acceptance criteria with extensive coverage; behaviors with many edge cases) - Very long: 300+ lines (rare; usually indicates the section should split into sub-sections)
Estimate based on the section's scope note from the Structure Outline. Sum the per-section estimates for a total ticket length estimate.
Cap-and-flag: if total estimate > 1500 lines, surface to the user — the ticket may be too large for one document. Per spec §2.3 heavy tickets can reach 1500+, but beyond that signals decomposition need (which is implementation-QRDSPIV's job, not ticket-writing's — log as a downstream concern).
Store as {{length_estimates}}: dict of section_name → lines.

substep.title: Define Review Checkpoints
substep.action:
Identify pause points where ticket-write should stop for user review:
- **Mandatory checkpoints**: - End of every Part (for heavy tickets with Parts) - End of every Critical-section (foundational sections like "Decisions" or "Behaviors" that influence later sections) - Before "Acceptance Criteria" is written (AC reads against everything before — user should approve the foundation first)
- **Optional checkpoints**: - Mid-Part if a Part exceeds 400 lines - After any section requiring subagent dispatch (so the user can verify the subagent findings landed correctly)
Each checkpoint is a {section_position, checkpoint_type} entry. Store as {{review_checkpoints}}.
For a single-Part flat ticket, mandatory checkpoints are: end-of-foundational-sections (Decisions), pre-AC, end-of-ticket. For multi-Part: end of each Part.

substep.title: Compose Plan Draft
Display Plan Summary

section.title: Why plan subagent dispatch at write-time
section.description:
Most subagent work happened in ticket-grill. But some sections of the ticket need fresh research that wasn't relevant during grilling. Example: the ticket may mention an API endpoint without yet listing every existing endpoint that this work will touch. The "API Surface" section needs a codebase-locator dispatch at write-time to enumerate them precisely.
Planning the dispatch now (in P) lets ticket-write parallelize — dispatch multiple sections' research subagents at once before writing begins.

rules.rule: Heavy tickets without checkpoints are unreviewable — the user can't hold the full ticket in head
Too many checkpoints fragment the writing flow; cap at one per ~300 lines of expected content
Always checkpoint before Acceptance Criteria — AC is the contract for downstream implementation, must be reviewed against the foundations
Always checkpoint at Part boundaries when Parts exist

section.title: Length estimates are guidance, not contracts
section.description: ticket-write treats estimates as targets. Sections that come in significantly under estimate (50% under) may be missing content; sections that come in significantly over (50% over) may have scope creep or should split. Both are signals ticket-verify will surface for review.
